title: Not So Strait-Forward: Hormuz, Iran, and the Future of Gulf Oil and Gas Flows | Council on Foreign Relations
gist: Reopening the Strait of Hormuz after a 100+ day shut-in (>10M bbl/d oil, ~300M m3/d LNG) is a phased engineering and geopolitical problem, not a switch; the roadmap is clear but true recovery is not, with Ras Laffan damage lasting up to five years, Iran/Oman seeking transit tolls, and buyers accelerating efforts to bypass the strait entirely.

themes.theme: Scale and sequencing of the disruption

insights.claim: There is no precedent for unwinding a market disruption of this magnitude—a shut-in equivalent to more than 10 million barrels per day of oil and roughly 300 million cubic meters per day of LNG sustained for over 100 days.
insights.speaker: Clara Gillispie
insights.so_what: Anchors the scale: even optimistic recovery scenarios have no historical template, so energy-price and shipping-cost forecasts carry unusually wide error bars.
insights.quote: there is simply no precedent for unwinding a market disruption of this magnitude

insights.claim: Restarting Gulf seaborne energy flows requires careful sequencing of four interdependent tasks—clearing trapped vessels, increasing inbound tankers, restarting production, and repairing damage—each with its own bottlenecks.
insights.speaker: Clara Gillispie
insights.so_what: A checklist for tracking recovery pace; progress on any single task without the others does not translate into restored volumes.
insights.locator: The outcome will depend on the various technical

insights.claim: Several hundred ships remained stuck inside the Gulf as of mid-June, and it typically takes about a month for tankers to transit from the Gulf to major destination markets in China, Japan, and South Korea.
insights.speaker: Clara Gillispie
insights.so_what: Even a rapid clearing produces a lag before barrels reach Asian buyers—physical delay between headlines and actual supply relief.

insights.claim: Restarting oil and gas production is a phased engineering process, not an on-off switch: well pressure must be adjusted gradually, and older fields in Iraq and Kuwait must proceed even more slowly and could in some cases struggle to come back online at all.
insights.speaker: Clara Gillispie
insights.so_what: Some pre-war production capacity may be permanently impaired—structurally bullish for prices at the margin versus a clean full recovery.

insights.claim: Damage to QatarEnergy's LNG production facilities at Ras Laffan is expected to take up to five years to repair, with additional gas-facility, port, and refinery damage across Iran, Saudi Arabia, Bahrain, Iraq, Kuwait, and the UAE.
insights.speaker: Clara Gillispie
insights.so_what: A multi-year LNG supply hit from Qatar tightens global gas markets well beyond the strait's reopening—bullish structural signal for LNG.

insights.claim: LNG export requires super-chilling gas into liquid—a process that typically takes about two weeks—and the liquefaction trains must be restarted sequentially, curtailing the pace of even a rapid LNG export restart from Qatar and others.
insights.speaker: Clara Gillispie
insights.so_what: Gas recovery mechanically lags oil recovery, so LNG spot markets stay tighter longer than crude.

themes.theme: Persistent risk: mines, tolls, and Iranian leverage

insights.claim: An estimated eighty mines remain in the strait's main navigation areas, and although the interim deal commits Iran to clearing them, the timeline is unsettled, forcing seaborne exports onto alternate routes closer to Iran and Oman that amplify those two states' influence over trade flows.
insights.speaker: Clara Gillispie
insights.so_what: Iran and Oman gain structural chokepoint power over routing—keeping insurance and geopolitical risk premia elevated indefinitely.
insights.locator: Even if the strait's gradual reopening continues

insights.claim: Despite Washington insisting the reopening be 'toll-free,' both Iran and Oman are moving forward with a plan to collect payments for guaranteeing safe passage out of the Strait of Hormuz, and Iran has already used force against ships transiting outside its preferred sea-lanes.
insights.speaker: Clara Gillispie
insights.so_what: A de facto Hormuz transit toll would be a permanent tax on Gulf-origin energy and a recurring flashpoint—watch for US countermeasures.

insights.claim: Even once export levels return to pre-closure levels, there is no guarantee they can be sustained while exports remain heavily reliant on seaborne trade and Iran retains the ability to close the strait at any time.
insights.speaker: Clara Gillispie
insights.so_what: A recurring closure-option keeps a persistent tail risk embedded in Gulf energy pricing regardless of any recovery snapshot.
insights.locator: Middle Eastern suppliers will now have to make

insights.claim: Kpler tracking showed seventy tankers sailed through the strait on June 25 alone, and QatarEnergy has begun a phased restart, saying it expects to restore roughly 80 percent of export capacity within two months.
insights.speaker: Clara Gillispie
insights.so_what: Hard evidence that stakeholders are already braving risk to restart flows—near-term supply is coming back faster than a pure risk model would suggest.

insights.claim: Gulf oil production could restabilize by the end of 2026 and put global markets back on track for an oil supply glut in 2027, per the IEA, while the gas outlook is less bullish but still supports a partial 2026 recovery.
insights.speaker: Clara Gillispie
insights.so_what: Base case points to oil oversupply by 2027—bearish for medium-term crude if the peace holds, with gas the tighter side of the trade.
insights.locator: Perhaps that is why the case

insights.claim: Buyers and producers are accelerating efforts to bypass Hormuz—the UAE is bringing forward the competition date of a planned overland pipeline—even as they accept higher insurance and shipping costs.
insights.speaker: Clara Gillispie
insights.so_what: Structural investment in bypass infrastructure is a durable theme—favors overland pipeline and non-Gulf supply plays over strait-dependent volumes.
insights.locator: To that end, even as buyers and producers

insights.claim: US and other producers are accelerating multi-year supply agreements with major Asian buyers to grab global market share, forcing Middle Eastern suppliers to prove both technical rebound and attractiveness against non-Gulf competition.
insights.speaker: Clara Gillispie
insights.so_what: The war may permanently shift market share from Gulf seaborne exporters to US and other suppliers via locked-in Asian offtake contracts.
insights.locator: Middle Eastern suppliers will now have to make

insights.claim: Chinese importers moved quickly to snatch up returning Gulf oil supply, buoying the demand signals needed to sustain a restart, while Indian buyers appear to still be hedging their bets.
insights.speaker: Clara Gillispie
insights.so_what: China provides the marginal demand pull sustaining restart; India's hesitancy is a swing factor for how fast Gulf volumes clear.
insights.locator: Early evidence shows that some traditional importers
